The Annual Leave Management Benchmark Report 2025

How real teams book, approve and actually take their time off
Based on a 2025 anonymised snapshot of Leave Dates customers · ~744k leave requests · 1.7M leave-days · figures suppress groups under 10

Executive summary

Across thousands of companies and 1.7 million leave-days, 2025 shows a fast, healthy leave operation — with a few quiet signals worth watching. The typical request is approved in under a working day (median 9.9 hours) and almost always granted (86% approved, under 2% refused). Employees hold a median of 20 days' allowance and use about 72% of it, leaving roughly 12 days each unspent. Leave clusters predictably — December and August peaks, long weekends bridged around public holidays — so the staffing crunches are foreseeable months ahead. But rest is increasingly fragmented: a third of employees never take a full working week off all year. And leave systems have quietly become presence systems, with working-from-home now logged as often as sick leave.

85.6%
of leave requests approved
9.9h

The big picture

What the year looks like across every Leave Dates customer: what people book, and how often it gets a yes.

What people actually book

Annual leave is two-thirds of everything

What it meansAnnual leave dominates, but the surprise is what sits behind it: working-from-home and remote-office days are now logged as their own categories and sit level with sick leave. Leave systems have quietly become presence systems — teams aren't just tracking who's away, but where everyone is working. Categories are normalised from 7,832 free-text names across a dozen languages; the 6.3% that couldn't be classified confidently sit in a clean "Other" rather than padding a real category.

Leave is rarely refused

7 cancellations for every rejection

What it meansOutright rejection is rare (1.5%). The real churn is cancellations (11%) — people changing their own plans, not managers saying no. For an employer that's a reassuring signal of trust, but it also means the calendar is more provisional than it looks: roughly one in nine booked days never happens.

Approvals & the weekend effect

How fast requests get a decision — and why the day you ask changes the answer's speed.

Most requests clear within a day

Median 9.9 hours · a third inside the hour

What it meansApproval is quick for most: a third are signed off within an hour and over six in ten within a day. The headline average (170 hours) is misleading — a small tail of very late approvals drags it up 17×. For benchmarking, the median is the honest number, and it says leave admin is rarely a bottleneck.

The weekend effect is real

Saturday requests wait 9× longer than midweek

What it meansApproval speed tracks approver availability, not policy. Requests made midweek are cleared in about five hours; those landing Friday-to-Sunday sit until Monday. Nobody is being ignored — the approver is simply off too. For employees the practical takeaway is plain: ask on a weekday and you'll hear back sooner.

Reactive leave clears fastest

Sick ~3h vs planned leave ~18-20h

What it meansApproval speed splits cleanly by the nature of the leave. Urgent, reactive types — sickness, dependant and compassionate leave — are signed off in around three hours, because managers know they can't wait. Planned types — annual leave, study, public-holiday bookings — sit far longer (8 to 20 hours), since there's no urgency to act. It's a healthy pattern: approvers triage by need, not by rule.

Approval culture varies widely

43% approve within 4h, but 15% take 3+ days

What it meansCompanies fall into two camps. Just over four in ten clear requests within four hours — a quick, responsive culture — while 15% routinely take three days or more. That slow tail is what drags the overall average; it's also the most fixable, since it's about process and delegation, not policy.

The Monday-9am bottleneck

Requests pile up Monday-Thursday mornings
Day \ hour01234567891011121314151617181920212223
Mon0.40.91.62.52.62.21.81.71.71.81.51.40.60.4
Tue0.71.21.92.21.81.71.51.61.71.40.90.60.4
Wed0.61.21.71.91.61.61.41.41.31.20.90.60.4
Thu0.61.01.61.81.51.31.31.21.31.30.90.5
Fri0.51.01.41.61.31.31.11.11.00.90.70.4
Sat0.5
Sun

What it meansMapping every request by the day and hour it was submitted, the single hottest moment of the week is Monday at 9am — people return, plan their time off, and book. Mornings Monday-to-Thursday carry the load; weekends are quiet. For approvers this is the predictable queue to staff around, and a clue for where quick-approve tools pay off most.

How people rest

Not just how much leave people take, but the shape of it — and whether it adds up to real rest.

A few real breaks, lots of odd days

Half of trips are single days — but they're only 18% of the time off

What it meansCounting requests makes leave look fragmented — half of all trips are a single day. But those single days are a small share of the actual time off. Most leave-days live in longer breaks: long weekends, the classic week away, the occasional fortnight. The single days are the noise; the holidays are the signal. Booking volume and rest are two different stories.

A third never fully switch off

34% never take a full working week in 2025

What it meansThis is the wellbeing flag. A third of employees never string together a full working week away, and under a quarter take a single ten-day break. Plenty of leave is being taken — just rarely in the long, continuous blocks that research links to genuine recovery. For HR, the prompt isn't "are people taking leave?" but "are they taking it in a way that restores them?"

Most leave is short

68% of requests are a day or less

What it meansShort, frequent absences are the norm — appointments, single days, the odd long weekend — while extended leave is the exception. That makes the calendar lumpy and hard to predict day-to-day, and it's why coverage planning matters more than headline allowance: the disruption comes from frequency, not from the occasional fortnight.

Timing & seasonality

When leave is taken across the year, and how teams turn public holidays into longer breaks.

Two peaks: December and August

December alone is 14.5% of the year's leave

What it meansAbsence is far from even. December is the standout — end-of-year holidays plus use-it-or-lose-it allowance burn — followed by the August summer break. February and November are the quiet troughs. For managers this is the planning spine of the year: the cover crunches are predictable months ahead, so the staffing risk is foreseeable rather than sudden.

Booked in January, taken in summer & December

Two different rhythms

What it meansPlanning and absence are out of phase. Annual leave is booked most in January — the new-year burst of locking in the year's holidays — but taken most in August and December. The gap means managers can see the year's pressure points early: the requests arrive months before the absences land, so the December crunch is visible in the booking data long before it happens.

Public holidays become long weekends

72% of companies show bridging behaviour
1.4×
more annual leave booked on the working days flanking a public holiday

What it meansPeople are strategic with their allowance. In nearly three-quarters of companies, the working days either side of a public holiday see about 1.4× the usual annual leave — a single booked day buys a four-day weekend. It's efficient for employees and entirely predictable for employers, which makes those bridge days easy to plan cover for.

Sickness & evidence

What sick-leave patterns really show — and when a sick note actually appears.

The 'Friday sickie' is a myth

Sick leave is lowest on Friday, highest on Monday

What it meansThe data flips a tired stereotype. Sickness peaks on Mondays and falls through the week to a Friday low, while annual leave does the opposite — people book Fridays off to extend weekends. So a Friday absence is far more likely planned holiday than a duvet day. Managers chasing "Friday sickies" are watching the wrong day.

Sick notes follow length, not policy

7% for a day → 26% for 8+ days

What it meansWhether evidence appears is driven by how long someone is off, not by company size or a blanket rule. A one-day absence almost never comes with a note; a week-plus increasingly does — the UK self-certification threshold showing up naturally in behaviour. Most short sickness runs on trust, which is worth remembering before adding documentation friction.

Evidence is a sick-and-civic thing

9% of sick leaves carry a document; 0.5% of annual

What it meansAttachments cluster exactly where you'd expect proof to matter — sickness and jury/civic duty — and are almost absent everywhere else. Even then, roughly nine in ten sick leaves carry no document at all. The takeaway for employers: supporting evidence is the exception, reserved for longer or formal absences, not a routine expectation across leave.

Explicit mental-health leave is rare

~0.1% of records — it hides inside sick leave

What it meansLeave explicitly labelled as mental-health is vanishingly small — about one in a thousand records, with a notable spike around April. That doesn't mean mental-health absence is rare; it means it's almost always logged as ordinary sick leave, because employees don't want to specify. These are the verbatim labels people and admins actually typed — e.g. “Mental Health Day”, “Wellbeing”, “Wellbeing day”, “Duvet Day”, “Self Care Day”, “Wellness Leave”, “🧘Duvet Day”, “Mental Health / Duvet” (filter the leave-type explorer to “MH only” to see all 34). The takeaway is to read the sick-leave figures knowing mental health sits inside them, largely invisible.

Allowances, usage & days lost

How generous allowances are, how much gets used, and how much quietly expires.

The typical allowance is 20 days

Most sit between 17 and 25 days

What it meansAnnual leave clusters tightly around the UK statutory-plus norm: a median of 20 days, with the bulk between 17 and 25. The long upper tail (30+ days) tends to be more generous or longer-tenured employers. As a benchmark, anything below ~17 days will read as lean to candidates comparing offers.

Polarised use — and days lost

A third take none; over 40% nearly max out

What it meansUsage splits into two camps: a large group uses almost all their allowance, while nearly a third take little or none — leaving roughly 12 days each unused. That's not always under-work; some is leave that simply can't be taken when cover is tight. Either way it's a hidden liability and a wellbeing risk, and it's why the December burn happens.

Carry-over is offered far more than used

45% can carry days; only 6% do

What it meansNearly half of employees have the right to roll days into next year, but only about one in sixteen actually does — and typically just a few days. Carry-over works mostly as a safety valve and a reassurance, not a routine habit. Generous policies here cost employers little in practice while removing a real source of end-of-year anxiety.

What an allowance is made of

Base entitlement plus a thin layer of extras

What it meansAn allowance isn't a single number — it's assembled from parts. Almost everyone (91%) has a base entitlement; a small minority add carry-over (6%), and very few have purchased days, long-service awards or adjustments. So when benchmarking generosity, the base entitlement is what matters — the extras are real but rarely move the total by much.

Overtime & time-off-in-lieu

A minority practice — but where it exists, a fast, lightweight 'bank it now, take it later' habit.

Niche, but heavily used where it lives

~1–2% of companies, but ~150k hours logged

What it meansOnly a small set of companies — often shift, field or operations-heavy — track overtime at all, but those that do use it intensively. It's logged in small, frequent increments and waved through almost instantly (90% approved, median 1.2 hours, far faster than leave). Every overtime company also runs a lieu allowance, so the pattern is clearly "bank the hours, take the time back later" rather than cash.

Steady through the year

Mild spring/summer rise, a December dip
1.2h
median time to approve overtime — versus 9.9 hours for leave

What it meansOvertime rises gently in spring and summer and dips in December — the mirror image of annual leave, which peaks then. The two balance each other: as people take time off at year-end, fewer extra hours are logged. The near-instant approval shows overtime is treated as routine record-keeping, not a decision to deliberate.

Who differs — size, region & leave type

The benchmark isn't one number. It shifts with company size, location and the kind of leave.

Bigger firms: more generous, slower

21→25 days, but 6→14 hours to approve

What it meansSize brings a clear trade-off. Larger companies offer more days (median rising from 21 to 25) but take longer to approve them as requests pass through more layers. Small firms are leaner on allowance but fast and personal. Neither is "better" — but it tells a growing company what tends to slip as it scales: speed.

Approval speed varies sharply by region

Europe 6h → Africa 24h

What it meansThe headline regional difference isn't generosity — entitlement is remarkably flat at ~20-21 days everywhere — it's how fast leave is signed off. Europe and the Pacific clear requests in about six hours; Asia and Africa take two-to-four times longer. That likely reflects approval-chain depth and working-hour overlap rather than policy, and it's the metric distributed teams should watch.

Regions at a glance

Generosity is flat; behaviour is not
RegionEmployeesEntitlement (d)Approval (h)Cancel %Same-day %
Europe121,52321611.310.4
America22,611201110.616
Australia4,99820108.614
Asia28,95220159.519
Africa8,21420249.521.5
Pacific1,438206529.4
Other1,528221810.812.7

What it meansSide by side, the regions tell one story: people are given roughly the same allowance everywhere (~20-21 days), but what they do with it and how it's managed differs. Approval is quick in Europe and slow in Africa; same-day (reactive) booking is far higher in Asia, Africa and the Pacific; cancellation rates sit in a narrow band. Cells are coloured on a cold-to-warm rainbow scale per column — cool blue is low, warm red is high — so you can read each metric at a glance. "Other" combines the small regions (Indian Ocean, Atlantic and unclassified timezones).

Each leave type has its own rhythm

Sick is 70% same-day; annual is planned a month out

What it meansLeave type shapes nearly every metric. Sick and dependant leave are overwhelmingly same-day and approved fastest — they're reactive by nature. Annual, study and parental leave are planned weeks ahead. Designing one approval workflow for all of them misses the point: the policy that suits a planned holiday is wrong for an emergency, and vice-versa.

Operational peak absence

Coverage risk concentrates on a few days
60%
of the workforce is off at once on the median company's single busiest day; 61% of companies cross 50%

What it meansAbsence isn't just an annual total — it clusters. On its peak day, the typical company has 60% of staff away simultaneously, and six in ten companies cross the 50% line at some point. That's the real operational risk hiding behind healthy-looking averages, and it lands on the predictable peaks — December and August — so it can be planned for.

Data wrangling — how the data was prepared

Every cleaning, normalisation and engineering decision behind the figures, so each number is fully traceable. This mirrors the methodology in the written report.

Data preparation & quality interrogation

Because this is the exploration phase, this section documents everything we did to the data and everything we noticed — how it was accessed, cleaned, joined, and where it needs care. All steps are reproducible in the accompanying Jupyter notebooks.

Scale & completeness
Leave-type normalisation

Each company defines its own leave categories, so the raw data held 7,833 distinct type labels (2,958 actually used in 2025). These were consolidated into a small set of clear categories. Working-from-home alone appeared under dozens of spellings (“WFH”, “Home Office”, “Working from Home”), and several non-English labels (e.g. “Szabadság”, “Concediu”) were mapped to English equivalents. About 5.3% stayed too ambiguous to classify and were grouped honestly as “Unmapped” rather than forced into a category. Presence markers (WFH/office) were separated from genuine absence so they don't distort leave metrics.

Linking the tables

The allowance extract initially carried only masked display labels with no keys, so it was re-exported with identifiers. The reliable join across leave, employees and allowances is leave.owner_id = employments.id = allowance.employment_id (verified at 100%). One important subtlety: most companies and employees are soft-deleted (churned customers), yet their 2025 data is valid — filtering them out would have dropped ~87% of companies, so they are retained and only flagged as churn.

Units & derived measures

Everything is stored in minutes, so days are derived as minutes ÷ minutes-per-working-day (handling companies that track in days vs hours, and full / half / custom day-parts). Approval latency is the gap between submission and the first genuine approval; weekends and public holidays inside a leave are excluded when counting working days.

Outliers & known issues to confirm
  • Long approval tail. About 1.7% of approvals (>90 days) and 2,344 beyond 180 days, max ~505 days. Likely retroactive or reopened entries; retained as a finding, not deleted, and excluded from the median.
  • Auto-approvals. 4.4% (19,317) are approved within a minute — probable auto-approval or bulk actions rather than manual review; worth separating from genuine response time.
  • Allowance outliers. Raw allowance values include impossible extremes (negative and tens of thousands of days), so entitlement is clipped to a plausible 0–70-day range.
  • Allowance with no base row. About 8.5% of employee-calendars carry an annual-leave allowance with no base-entitlement breakdown (recorded only via carry-over or other components). Retained but flagged for the product team to confirm.
  • Rejection reasons not stored here. The leave `reason` field is essentially empty; rejection/cancellation reasons live in the timeline-events table (a pending export).
  • Timezone basis. The clean Monday-9am submission peak suggests timestamps are local time, but this should be confirmed before scheduling conclusions are drawn.

How leave types were categorised

Leave Dates lets every company name its own leave types, so the raw data is not a tidy list of categories but 7,832 distinct free-text labels — written in at least a dozen languages and peppered with codes, initials, emojis and typos. ‘Annual Leave’, ‘Szabadság’, ‘Congés Payés (CP)’, ‘Vakantie’ and ‘Férias’ all mean the same thing; ‘Thuiswerk’ and ‘WFH’ both mean working from home. Before any leave-type figure can be trusted, these labels have to be normalised to a common set of categories.

A two-pass classification model

The first pass is an AI semantic classifier that reads each label in context and assigns it to one of eighteen categories. Because it works from meaning rather than spelling, it handles the multilingual names and their translation automatically — mapping, for example, the Hungarian ‘Szabadság’ and French ‘Congés Payés’ to Annual Leave without a separate translation step. The second pass is a transparent, auditable keyword rule-set: a dictionary of multilingual word-stems (such as wfh / thuiswerk / télétravail → Work-from-home, and sick / krank / malade / ziek → Sick) that fills gaps the first pass left blank and corrects obvious misreads.

How the two passes are combined — and the ‘Other’ rule

The passes are merged conservatively. A keyword match takes precedence where it fires; otherwise the semantic label stands; and a confident label is never downgraded. A name keeps a specific category only when the result is confident — when the two passes agree, or the semantic pass makes a clear call. Anything contested between the methods, weakly signalled, or genuinely unmappable (office codes, initials, one-off labels) is placed in a single ‘Other’ bucket rather than guessed. The principle is to under-claim rather than to inflate a category such as sick leave with ambiguous entries.

After this process about 94% of leave records carry a confident category and roughly 6% fall into Other. The full mapping — all 7,832 raw names, their final category, the reason any name was routed to Other, and both passes’ suggestions — is provided in the accompanying workbook, Leave_Dates_Leave_Type_Categories.xlsx, so every figure in this section is fully traceable and reproducible against next year’s data.

Points to confirm with you

A few naming conventions are judgement calls worth confirming before publication. First, in UK usage ‘holiday’ means annual leave, so labels such as ‘HOL’, ‘Holiday’ and ‘Holidays’ are counted as Annual Leave; only labels that explicitly say ‘bank holiday’ or ‘public holiday’, or name a specific day (Christmas, Easter), are counted as Public Holiday. Second, a small number of low-volume abbreviations were assigned by best-guess where no clear signal existed — for example ‘ROL’, read as annual leave but possibly ‘rolled-over leave’. These do not materially affect any headline figure, but the full decision trail (and every name placed in ‘Other’) is listed in the accompanying workbook for review.

Other engineered & derived fields

Leave-type categories are not the only values we computed rather than read straight from the database. For full transparency, every engineered field is listed below with its exact rule, and the complete raw-to-group mappings are provided in the accompanying workbook, Leave_Dates_Engineered_Fields.xlsx. Two of these — region and company size — involve an inference and are worth confirming; the rest are deterministic transformations.

Region (inferred from timezone)

There is no country field in the data, so each employee's region is inferred from their IANA timezone: the part before the slash is mapped to a continent — Europe/London becomes Europe, America/New_York becomes America, Asia/Kolkata becomes Asia, and so on; blanks become Unknown. This is reliable in aggregate but imperfect for remote or relocated staff (someone in a London timezone is counted as Europe regardless of where they sit). 320 distinct timezones map to seven regions; the full list is in the workbook.

Company size band

There is no stored headcount, so company size is the count of non-deleted employments per company, bucketed into 1–10, 11–50, 51–200 and 200+. Churned (soft-deleted) companies are kept in the analysis; only deleted employee records are excluded from the headcount. Every company's headcount and band is listed in the workbook.

Allowance components (grouped from free text)

An allowance is built from sub-rows whose type is free text — 1,834 distinct labels such as ‘Leave Entitlement’, ‘Carry Over’, ‘Buy / Sell’, ‘Long Service’. These are grouped by rule into five components: labels containing ‘long service’ → long service; ‘buy’, ‘sell’ or ‘purchas’ → purchased; ‘carr’ (or policy-type 2) → carry-over; ‘adjust’ or ‘expire’ → adjustment; everything else → base entitlement. The full breakdown-type-to-component mapping is in the workbook.

Derived measures (deterministic)

The remaining engineered fields are straight calculations. Days are derived from minutes as amount-in-minutes ÷ minutes-per-working-day (which absorbs the difference between day-unit and hour-unit companies). Allowance usage is annual-leave days taken ÷ total available allowance (base + carry-over + purchased + long service + adjustment), reported in bands (took zero / 1–49% / 50–89% / 90%+). Time-to-approval is the first genuine approval minus submission, with approvals under one minute excluded as auto/bulk and the median reported rather than the mean. Notice period is leave start minus booking date. ‘Trips’ stitch consecutive leave days into one break when they are three or fewer calendar days apart. A day is classed full / half / hours from the breakdown record. All bucket thresholds are listed in the workbook’s ‘Bucket definitions’ tab.

Data explorer — leave-type categories

Every one of the 7,832 distinct leave-type names companies typed, and the category each maps to. Each row is one name; the “2025 uses” column is the record count for that name, and those sum to the ~744k total leave records (many defined names had zero bookings in 2025). Search, filter and sort to verify any figure; click a column header to sort. The Mental-health flag column marks the 34 wellbeing-related names behind the 0.1% figure — set it to “MH only” to see them all.

Leave name (raw)Category2025 usesAI suggestionKeyword suggestionMental-health flag
No cards match your search. Try a broader term, or clear the filter.
About this benchmark. Figures are drawn from an anonymised 2025 snapshot of Leave Dates customer data, covering roughly 744,000 leave requests and 1.7 million leave-days. All metrics are aggregated; very small groups are treated with caution because they are statistically unreliable (this floor also future-proofs the planned company-vs-benchmark view). As the data is already anonymised, this is a reliability safeguard, not a privacy measure — and in practice every figure shown is built from hundreds to hundreds-of-thousands of records.

A note on method. Time-to-approval and notice use the median, not the mean, because a small number of very late actions distort averages. "Trips" stitch consecutive leave days (bridging weekends) into real breaks. Allowance usage covers the ~5,700 companies that actively booked leave in 2025. Leave-type categories are normalised from 7,832 free-text names (in a dozen-plus languages) using a two-pass classifier — an AI semantic pass plus a transparent multilingual keyword pass; only confident labels keep a category, and the rest fall into "Other" (~6%). A few sections — reasons for cancellation/rejection, and industry-level breakdowns — are not shown here because the underlying fields are removed by the privacy masking on this dataset.
